## CAA Approval

# CAA Approval

![](data:image/png;base64,)


---

## 0 - Administration and Control of Operations Manua

# Introduction

The Operations Manual is for use of operations personnel and contains or refers to the policies, procedures, checklists, instructions and other guidelines or information necessary for compliance with applicable regulations, laws, rules and Oman Air standards.

The Operations Manual (OM) Parts A, B, and C are issued on the authority of the Nominated Post Holder Flight Operations. The Operations Manual (OM) Part D is issued on the authority of the Nominated Postholder Crew Training.

The contents of the Operations Manual have been prepared in accordance with Appendix 1 to CAR-OPS 1.1045.

All information provided in the Operations Manual shall be treated as Company property and must not be transferred to third parties without formal approval of Oman Air.

The Operations Manual is approved by the CAA in accordance with CAR-OPS 1.

English language is the designated common operational language for all flight operations. Documentation used in the conduct or support of flight operations, including the Operations Manual is written in English language. English language shall be used for all operational communications on the flight deck during line operations; between flight crew and cabin crew during line operations; and during flight crew training and evaluation activities.


---

## 1 - Organisation and Responsibilities

# Organisational Structure

This subsection and documents referred herein, describe the organisational structure including the general Company organigram and operations department organigram. The organigram depicts the relationship between operations department and other department of the Company. In particular, the subordination and reporting lines of all divisions, departments etc. which pertain to the safety and security of Flight Operations are shown.


---

## 2 - Operational Control and Supervision

# Supervision of the Operation by the Operator

**_General_**

The Company complies with the standards set through a sound, effective and competent management structure.

Oman Air is authorised to conduct commercial air transportation under, and in accordance with, the terms and conditions of Air Operator Certificate (AOC) issued by the CAA. The AOC authorises Oman Air to conduct air transport operations in accordance with the Operations Specifications issued by the CAA.

The Company's AOC is granted by the CAA on the basis of the rules and requirements described in the Company's Operations Manual, the Company operational management organization including the quality assurance system is responsible for exercising control and supervision of operations conducted under the terms of the AOC.

The management structure is set out in [OM-A 1](javascript:void\(0\);) and the nominated post holders are named in with their duties and responsibilities in [OM-A 1](javascript:void\(0\);).

**_Method of Supervision_**

Oman Air has employed competent (as defined in _[OM-A 1](javascript:void\(0\);)_) management personnel ("Nominated Post Holders" accepted by CAA) in key positions on a full-time basis to ensure the highest degree of safety in its operations. Notice in writing of any intended or actual change in appointments or functions will be given to the CAA. Other than in exceptional circumstances, CAA must be given at least 10 days prior notice of a proposed change of a Nominated Post Holder (NPH).

Oman Air has also ensured continuity of supervision in the absence of Nominated Post Holders in respective functions.

A person nominated as a post holder by the holder of an AOC must not be nominated as a post holder by the holder of any other AOC, unless acceptable to the CAA.

Nominated Post Holders must be contracted to work sufficient hours such that the individual can fulfil the management functions associated with the size and scope of the Airline's business.

More than one of the nominated posts may be filled by one person if acceptable to the CAA.

_Oversight_

In cases where the Oman Air contracts other organisations to provide certain services, it shall retain responsibility for the maintenance of proper standards. In such circumstances, the Nominated Post Holder shall ensure that contractor employed meets the required standards, and develop associated policies and procedures.

_Adequacy and Supervision of Staff_

Oman Air has employed sufficient flight and cabin crew for the planned operation, and they are trained and checked in accordance with the requirements of the Operations Manual and CAA regulations.

Oman Air has employed sufficient ground staff to meet the requirements of the nature and scale of operations contemplated. Operations and ground handling departments, in particular, shall be staffed by trained personnel who have a thorough understanding of their responsibilities within the organisation.

Oman Air shall also ensure that the number of supervisors to be appointed is matched to the organisational structure and the number of staff employed. The duties and responsibilities of these supervisors shall be as defined in the Operations Manual. Any flying commitments shall be arranged in such a way that the flying personnel have adequate time to discharge their supervisory responsibilities.

Oman Air shall ensure that supervision of all crew members and ground staff is exercised by individuals possessing experience and personal qualities to ensure the attainment of standards specified in the Operations Manual.

_Mental and Physical Fitness_

Postholders shall ensure that personnel who perform operationally critical functions remain physically and mentally fit for duty.

Flight operation and cabin management, and operational personnel holding a current aviation medical certificate are considered mentally and physically fit for their duties.

Determining the usability of aerodromes, the establishment of the aerodrome operating and planning minima, minimum flight altitude, routes to be planned and areas to be over flown for the proposed flights are all required to be approved by VP Flight Operations. These aspects are thoroughly checked by Integrated Operations Control prior to release of the flight.

**The above aspects have been complied by Oman Air with the designation of competent management team to supervise operations activities.**

**_Accommodation Facilities_**

Oman Air has ensured that working space at each operating base is sufficient for personnel pertaining to the safety of flight operations. Consideration has been given to the needs of ground staff, those concerned with operational control, and the storage and display of essential records.

Office services are capable, without delay, of distributing operational instructions and other information to all concerned.


---

## 3 - Quality System

# Quality System

Oman Air has established an Operational Quality System and has designated Function Head - Quality Assurance to monitor compliance with, and adequacy of, Civil Aviation Law, International Civil Aviation Organisation, Civil Aviation Regulations and Civil Aviation Notices, International Air Transport Standards and Recommended Practices, International Organisation for standardisation, Industries Best Practices, International Organisation for standardization, Industries Best Practices and Oman Air & Internal Standards, etc. to ensure safe operational practices.

The detailed description, processes and procedures of the Quality System are documented in the Oman Air Quality Assurance Operations Manual.


---

## 4 - Crew Composition

# Type of Aeroplane

**_Flight Crew_**

For any flight, the number and composition of the flight crew must never be less than the number required by the AFM.

For normal operations all Oman Air aircraft require a minimum of 2 pilots consisting of Commander and Co-pilot who are suitably qualified and competent to conduct the duties assigned to them.

_Pilots Aged 60 or Older_

Pilot aged 60-64 years must not act as a pilot of an aircraft engaged in commercial air transport except:

  1. As a member of a multi-pilot crew; and

  2. Provided that such a holder is the only pilot in the flight crew who has attained the age of 60 years.




Pilot aged of 65 years or more years shall not act as a pilot of an aircraft engaged in commercial air transport.

_Safety Pilot_

A safety pilot is a pilot who is qualified to act as PIC on the class/type of aeroplane and carried on board the aeroplane, which is fitted with dual controls for the purpose of taking over control should the PIC holding this specific medical certificate restriction become incapacitated. The following information shall assist persons acting as safety pilots:

  1. The background for establishing the role of a safety pilot;

  2. The logging of flight time whilst acting as a safety pilot;

  3. The types of medical condition which restrict a particular pilot from flying solo;

  4. The safety pilot's role and responsibilities;

  5. Guidance material to assist the safety pilot in the conduct of this role.




Note:

whenever a pilot license holder with a safety pilot restriction renews or is issued with the related medical certificate, the holder should receive from CAA an information sheet. This sheet will give advice to pilots utilised by the license holder in the capacity of safety pilot.

**_Cabin Crew_**

_Regulatory Minimum Cabin Crew Composition_

The minimum number of cabin crew required for a specific aircraft is based on CAR-OPS 1.990. For carrying revenue passengers, the regulatory minimum number of cabin crew for each aircraft type/variant operated by Oman Air is given in the following table.

Table 4.1.1-1 Cabin Crew Complement Aircraft Type |  Regulatory Minimum  
---|---  
A330-300 | 8  
A330-200 | 8  
B787-8 | 8  
B787-9 | 8  
9 (If passenger suites are installed and used)  
B737-800/900ER | 4  
B737-800BCF | Refer [OM-A 14.2](javascript:void\(0\);)  
B737-8 | 4  
  
Note:

1: Minimum cabin crew complement includes Cabin Director.

2: A Trainee Cabin Crew (Supernumerary) shall not be considered as a part of the minimum cabin crew complement.

3: When no revenue passengers are carried, upto 19 non-revenue occupants may be on board with a at least one operating qualified cabin crew member with a minimum of one year flying experience on type.

Cabin crew members shall wear uniform in accordance with guidelines established by the Company. The wearing of sharp metal objects (such as pins) without shielding of the needle point is not permitted.

_Below Regulatory Minimum Cabin Crew_

In unforeseen circumstances the required number of cabin crew may be reduced with the following conditions:

  1. Operations with below regulatory minimum cabin crew is not allowed for flight from base [Muscat];

  2. At least one cabin crew member is required for every 50, or fraction of 50, passengers present in the aircraft;

  3. Re-seating of passengers with due regard to exits and other applicable aeroplane limitations;

  4. Remaining cabin crew duties to be re-allocated to cover the cabin;

  5. Able-bodied persons seated near the exits to be briefed on the evacuation procedures;

  6. Upon completion of the flight, the Commander shall submit an report to the Manager Fleet. The report should be submitted by email to FL-FC@omanair.com and should include the following details:

     1. Flight Number

     2. Date of Flight

     3. Type of aircraft

     4. Registration

     5. Sector (From/To)

     6. Total Number of Cabin Crew (actual reduced complement)

     7. Vacant Cabin Crew Position (L1, R1...)

     8. Description of circumstances and remarks

![](https://oma.orlandotechpubs.com/./images/pdf-dl.png)

The pdf form can be used by the Commander to complete the report.




Upon receipt of the report from the Commander, the concerned Manager Fleet will review it, add his remarks and send it to the office of VPFO for further submission to the CAA-Director of Flight Safety.

_Arabic Speaker_

Arabic speaking cabin crew should be included on board every flight departing from home base.


---

## 5 - Qualification Requirements

# Description

This section contains a description of the required licenses, ratings, qualifications (e.g., for route and aerodrome), experience, courses, training, checking and recency for operations personnel to conduct their duties.

The minimum qualifications, experience and evaluation requirements for flight and cabin crew training, checking and supervisory personnel are detailed in [OM-D 2](javascript:void\(0\);) and [OM-D 10](javascript:void\(0\);).

All crew members have to carry with them, the required licenses/certificates to exercise their duties, as detailed in _[OM-A 8.1.12.2](javascript:void\(0\);)_.

Although Oman Air shall ensure that all crew members are qualified and current in accordance with the requirements before scheduling them for a duty by using the procedures of the rostering and AIMS-Crewing Computer system, the crew members are also responsible for the renewal of their licenses/certificates.

All crewmembers shall hold an applicable and valid license acceptable to the CAA and must be suitably qualified and competent to conduct the duties assigned to them. The holder of license or rating shall not exercise privileges other than those granted by that licence or rating.

A licence holder shall not exercise the privileges granted by any licence or rating unless the holder maintains competency by meeting all the relevant requirements which includes recurrent training/checks as applicable.

The validity of the licence is determined by the validity of the ratings therein and the medical certificate.


---

## 6 - Crew Health Precautions

# Crew Health Precautions

License holders shall not exercise the privileges of their license and related ratings or certificates at any time when they:

  1. are aware of any decrease in their medical fitness which might render them unable to safely and properly exercise his privileges;

  2. have knowingly been in contact with another person who has subsequently tested positive to any highly infectious virus or transmittable disease prior to commencing a flight duty period (refer _[OM-A 6.1.14](javascript:void\(0\);))_

  3. take or use any prescribed or non-prescribed medication which is likely to interfere with the safe exercise of the privileges to the applicable license;

  4. receive any medical, surgical or other treatment (such as acupuncture, homeopathy, hypnotherapy and several other disciplines) that is likely to interfere with flight safety.




License holder shall, without undue delay seek aero-medical advice (AME) when they:

  1. have undergone a surgical operation or invasive procedure;

  2. have commenced the regular use of any medication;

  3. have suffered any significant personal injury involving incapacity to function as a member of the flight crew;

  4. have been suffering from any significant illness involving incapacity to function as a member of the flight crew;

  5. are pregnant;

  6. have been admitted to hospital or medical clinic;

  7. first require correcting lenses;

  8. Transient Ischemic Attack;

  9. coronary angiography;

  10. abnormal heart rhythms including atrial fibrillation/flutter;

  11. have any loss of consciousness.




Crew members are reminded that they are not entitled to act as an operating crewmember of an aircraft if they know or suspect that their physical or mental condition renders them temporarily unfit so to act.

A crew member shall not perform duties on an aeroplane if he is in any doubt of being able to accomplish his assigned duties or if he knows or suspects that he is suffering from fatigue, or feels unfit to the extent that the flight may be endangered.

**_Use of Psychoactive Substances_**

Crewmembers and operational control personnel shall not intent to exercise and/or exercise the privileges of their licenses and related rating while under the influence of any psychoactive substance which might render them unable to safely and properly exercise these privileges.

Crewmembers or any other operational control personnel shall not engage in any problematic use of substances.

Note:

**Psychoactive Substances:** Substances that can produce mood changes or distorted perceptions in humans to include, alcohol, opioids, cannabinoids, sedatives and hypnotics, cocaine, other psychostimulants, hallucinogens and volatile solvents; coffee and tobacco are excluded.

**Problematic Use of Substances:** The use of one or more psychoactive substances by aviation personnel in a way that

  * constitutes a direct hazard to the user or endangers the lives, health or welfare of others, and /or

  * causes or worsens an occupational social, mental or physical problem or disorder.




All applicants for CAA initial medical certification are required to undertake a pre-employment psychoactive substance screening test.

Initial applicant for CAA medical certification and CAA license holders shall always submit to an Alcohol screening or Drug screening when they are asked to do so by their Employer, AME, recognized laboratory or CAA. Refusal to Take Test by an employee will constitute a positive test result.

Crewmembers shall be subjected to random test to indicate the use of alcohol and/or psychoactive substances.

Crewmembers may be subjected to reasonable suspicion testing to indicate the use of alcohol and/or psychoactive substances.

Crewmembers or any other operational control personnel found under influence of psychoactive substances will be suspended from further duties until completion of investigation and faces possible termination for further employment with the Company.


---

## 7 - Flight Time Limitations

# General

Oman Air flight and duty time limitations (FDTL) and rest scheme for crew members has been developed in accordance with the provisions of CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.

Flights shall be planned to be completed within the allowable flight duty period taking into account the time necessary for pre-flight duties, taxiing, the flight, the sector, the turnaround times, and the nature of operation.

Duty rosters shall be prepared and published Seven (07) days in advance with planning of recurrent extended recovery rest periods (Days Off) to provide the opportunity for crew members to plan adequate pre-duty rest.

**Every crew member shall strictly adhere to the provisions of this scheme. They have equal responsibility for preventing the onset of fatigue.**

**A crew member shall not operate on an aircraft if he knows or suspects that he is suffering from or is likely to suffer from fatigue, or feels unfit to the extent that the flight may be endangered.**

**A crew member shall not be required to fly, if there is any reason to believe that he/ she is suffering, or is likely to suffer while flying, from such fatigue as may endanger the safety of the aircraft or of its occupants.**

**Crew members are reminded that they are not entitled to act as an operating crew member of an aircraft if they know or suspect that their physical or mental condition renders them temporarily unfit so to act.**

Crew members shall make optimum use of the opportunities and facilities for rest provided and plan and use these rest periods properly.

Oman Air shall use calendar month as the method of determining all flight and duty time limitations.

**Away from base, the crew members shall be provided both the opportunity and facilities for adequate pre-flight rest in suitable accommodation.**

Training of rostering staff shall include guidance on the effects of disturbing circadian rhythms and sleep deprivation.

Crew Planning and Control Section shall ensure that Crew schedule and/or crew arrangements is changed if the actual operation exceeds the maximum flight duty period on more than 33 percent of the flight duties in that schedule during a scheduled seasonal period.

When developing a workable roster, there shall be a fair balance between the commercial needs and the capacity of individual crew members to work effectively. Rosters shall be developed in such a way that they distribute the amount of work evenly among those that are involved.

**_General Principles_**

The prime objective of a flight and duty time limitation scheme shall ensure that crew members are adequately rested at the beginning of each flying duty period (FDP), and whilst flying are sufficiently free from fatigue so that they can operate to a satisfactory level of efficiency and safety under all circumstances and in all normal and abnormal situations.

The relationship between the freqency and pattern of flight duty periods and rest periods shall be taken into account and consideration given to the cumulative effects of undertaking long duty hours combined with minimum rest periods.

Factors to be considered when planning duty periods shall include;

  1. The allocation of work patterns, which avoid such undesirable practices as;

     1. alternating day/night duties ([OM-A 7.1.6.1](javascript:void\(0\);));

     2. the positioning of crews so that a serious disruption of established sleep/work patterns occur ([OM-A 7.1.6.7](javascript:void\(0\);));

     3. alternating eastward-westward or westward-eastward time zone transitions;

     4. the scheduling of rest periods between 18 and 30 hours especially after long flights crossing multiple time zones ([OM-A 7.1.6.9](javascript:void\(0\);)).

  2. Planning days off and notifying crews well in advance.

  3. Internal consultation with relevant stakeholders to agree basic roster concepts, which ensure adequate rest prior to flight.




**_Operational Robustness of Rosters_**

Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should be established and monitored.

The performance indicators for operational robustness should support in assessment of the stability of the rostering system. Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should at least measure how often a rostered crew pairing for a duty period is achieved within the planned duration of that duty period.

Note:

Crew pairing means rostered positioning and flights for crew members in one duty period.


---

## 7.1 - Flight Time Limitation

# General

Oman Air flight and duty time limitations (FDTL) and rest scheme for crew members has been developed in accordance with the provisions of CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.

Flights shall be planned to be completed within the allowable flight duty period taking into account the time necessary for pre-flight duties, taxiing, the flight, the sector, the turnaround times, and the nature of operation.

Duty rosters shall be prepared and published Seven (07) days in advance with planning of recurrent extended recovery rest periods (Days Off) to provide the opportunity for crew members to plan adequate pre-duty rest.

**Every crew member shall strictly adhere to the provisions of this scheme. They have equal responsibility for preventing the onset of fatigue.**

**A crew member shall not operate on an aircraft if he knows or suspects that he is suffering from or is likely to suffer from fatigue, or feels unfit to the extent that the flight may be endangered.**

**A crew member shall not be required to fly, if there is any reason to believe that he/ she is suffering, or is likely to suffer while flying, from such fatigue as may endanger the safety of the aircraft or of its occupants.**

**Crew members are reminded that they are not entitled to act as an operating crew member of an aircraft if they know or suspect that their physical or mental condition renders them temporarily unfit so to act.**

Crew members shall make optimum use of the opportunities and facilities for rest provided and plan and use these rest periods properly.

Oman Air shall use calendar month as the method of determining all flight and duty time limitations.

**Away from base, the crew members shall be provided both the opportunity and facilities for adequate pre-flight rest in suitable accommodation.**

Training of rostering staff shall include guidance on the effects of disturbing circadian rhythms and sleep deprivation.

Crew Planning and Control Section shall ensure that Crew schedule and/or crew arrangements is changed if the actual operation exceeds the maximum flight duty period on more than 33 percent of the flight duties in that schedule during a scheduled seasonal period.

When developing a workable roster, there shall be a fair balance between the commercial needs and the capacity of individual crew members to work effectively. Rosters shall be developed in such a way that they distribute the amount of work evenly among those that are involved.

**_General Principles_**

The prime objective of a flight and duty time limitation scheme shall ensure that crew members are adequately rested at the beginning of each flying duty period (FDP), and whilst flying are sufficiently free from fatigue so that they can operate to a satisfactory level of efficiency and safety under all circumstances and in all normal and abnormal situations.

The relationship between the freqency and pattern of flight duty periods and rest periods shall be taken into account and consideration given to the cumulative effects of undertaking long duty hours combined with minimum rest periods.

Factors to be considered when planning duty periods shall include;

  1. The allocation of work patterns, which avoid such undesirable practices as;

     1. alternating day/night duties ([OM-A 7.1.6.1](javascript:void\(0\);));

     2. the positioning of crews so that a serious disruption of established sleep/work patterns occur ([OM-A 7.1.6.7](javascript:void\(0\);));

     3. alternating eastward-westward or westward-eastward time zone transitions;

     4. the scheduling of rest periods between 18 and 30 hours especially after long flights crossing multiple time zones ([OM-A 7.1.6.9](javascript:void\(0\);)).

  2. Planning days off and notifying crews well in advance.

  3. Internal consultation with relevant stakeholders to agree basic roster concepts, which ensure adequate rest prior to flight.




**_Operational Robustness of Rosters_**

Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should be established and monitored.

The performance indicators for operational robustness should support in assessment of the stability of the rostering system. Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should at least measure how often a rostered crew pairing for a duty period is achieved within the planned duration of that duty period.

Note:

Crew pairing means rostered positioning and flights for crew members in one duty period.


---

## 7.1.1 - General

# General

Oman Air flight and duty time limitations (FDTL) and rest scheme for crew members has been developed in accordance with the provisions of CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.

Flights shall be planned to be completed within the allowable flight duty period taking into account the time necessary for pre-flight duties, taxiing, the flight, the sector, the turnaround times, and the nature of operation.

Duty rosters shall be prepared and published Seven (07) days in advance with planning of recurrent extended recovery rest periods (Days Off) to provide the opportunity for crew members to plan adequate pre-duty rest.

**Every crew member shall strictly adhere to the provisions of this scheme. They have equal responsibility for preventing the onset of fatigue.**

**A crew member shall not operate on an aircraft if he knows or suspects that he is suffering from or is likely to suffer from fatigue, or feels unfit to the extent that the flight may be endangered.**

**A crew member shall not be required to fly, if there is any reason to believe that he/ she is suffering, or is likely to suffer while flying, from such fatigue as may endanger the safety of the aircraft or of its occupants.**

**Crew members are reminded that they are not entitled to act as an operating crew member of an aircraft if they know or suspect that their physical or mental condition renders them temporarily unfit so to act.**

Crew members shall make optimum use of the opportunities and facilities for rest provided and plan and use these rest periods properly.

Oman Air shall use calendar month as the method of determining all flight and duty time limitations.

**Away from base, the crew members shall be provided both the opportunity and facilities for adequate pre-flight rest in suitable accommodation.**

Training of rostering staff shall include guidance on the effects of disturbing circadian rhythms and sleep deprivation.

Crew Planning and Control Section shall ensure that Crew schedule and/or crew arrangements is changed if the actual operation exceeds the maximum flight duty period on more than 33 percent of the flight duties in that schedule during a scheduled seasonal period.

When developing a workable roster, there shall be a fair balance between the commercial needs and the capacity of individual crew members to work effectively. Rosters shall be developed in such a way that they distribute the amount of work evenly among those that are involved.

**_General Principles_**

The prime objective of a flight and duty time limitation scheme shall ensure that crew members are adequately rested at the beginning of each flying duty period (FDP), and whilst flying are sufficiently free from fatigue so that they can operate to a satisfactory level of efficiency and safety under all circumstances and in all normal and abnormal situations.

The relationship between the freqency and pattern of flight duty periods and rest periods shall be taken into account and consideration given to the cumulative effects of undertaking long duty hours combined with minimum rest periods.

Factors to be considered when planning duty periods shall include;

  1. The allocation of work patterns, which avoid such undesirable practices as;

     1. alternating day/night duties ([OM-A 7.1.6.1](javascript:void\(0\);));

     2. the positioning of crews so that a serious disruption of established sleep/work patterns occur ([OM-A 7.1.6.7](javascript:void\(0\);));

     3. alternating eastward-westward or westward-eastward time zone transitions;

     4. the scheduling of rest periods between 18 and 30 hours especially after long flights crossing multiple time zones ([OM-A 7.1.6.9](javascript:void\(0\);)).

  2. Planning days off and notifying crews well in advance.

  3. Internal consultation with relevant stakeholders to agree basic roster concepts, which ensure adequate rest prior to flight.




**_Operational Robustness of Rosters_**

Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should be established and monitored.

The performance indicators for operational robustness should support in assessment of the stability of the rostering system. Performance indicators for operational robustness of rosters should at least measure how often a rostered crew pairing for a duty period is achieved within the planned duration of that duty period.

Note:

Crew pairing means rostered positioning and flights for crew members in one duty period.


---

## 7.1.2 - Definitions

# Definitions

For the purpose of this chapter (OM-A 7), the following definitions shall apply:

**Acclimatised**
    

When a crew member has spent 3 consecutive local nights on the ground within a local time zone band, which is two hours wide, and is able to take uninterrupted night's sleep. The crew member will remain acclimatised thereafter until a duty period finishes at a place where local time differs by more than 2 hours from that at the point of departure.

**Accommodation (also see - Suitable Accommodation)**
    

For the purpose of standby and split duty, a quiet and comfortable place not open to the public with the ability to control light and temperature, equipped with adequate furniture that provides a crew member with the possibility to sleep, with enough capacity to accommodate all crew members present at the same time and with access to food and drink. For guidance refer [OM-A 7.1.2.1](javascript:void\(0\);).

**Actual Flight Operation**
    

Actual flight operation starts at the reporting time and ends when the crew goes off duty.

**Adequate Facilities**
    

A quiet and comfortable place not open to the public.

**Augmented Flight Crew**
    

A flight crew that comprises more than the minimum number required to operate the aeroplane and in which each flight crew member can leave his or her assigned post and be replaced by another appropriately qualified flight crew member for the purpose of in-flight rest.

**Block Time**
    

The time between the time an aircraft first moves from its parking place for the purpose of taking off until it comes to rest on the designated parking position or until all engines are stopped.

**Break**
    

A period of time within a flight duty period, shorter than a rest period, counting as duty and during which a crew member is free of all tasks.

**Cabin Crew Member**
    

An appropriately qualified crew member, other than a flight crew member, who is assigned by the Company to perform duty related to safety of flight and passengers during operations of aircraft.

**Commander (the pilot in command)**
    

The pilot designated by the Company being in command and charged with the safe conduct of a flight.

**Contactable**
    

A short period of time during the day, other than a day off, during which the Company requires a crew member to be at an agreed location for the purpose of giving notification of a duty period, which will commence not less than 10 hours ahead.

Notification of rostered duties and changes thereof is given to the crew by providing them with published rosters and change information through AIMS. When it is required to otherwise contact the crew to notify them for any change in duties, it shall be ensured that it does not disrupt thier sleep and minimum rest periods. Refer OM-A 7.1.6.9 - Modes of Notification.

**Crew Member**
    

A person assigned by the Company to perform duty on an aircraft.

**Days Off**
    

Periods of relaxation free from all duties. A single day off shall include two local nights (minimum of 34 hours period). Consecutive days off shall include a further local night for each additional consecutive day off. A rest period may be included as part of a day off.

**Delayed Reporting**
    

Means the postponement of a scheduled FDP by the operator before a crew member has left the place of rest.

**Duty**
    

Any task that crew member is required by the Company to perform, including, for example, flight duty, administrative work, training, positioning and standby when it is likely to induce fatigue. Administrative work has to be understood as any task that a crew member is required to carry out associated with the business of the airline (AOC holder). It includes:

  * time spent in the office on official duties;

  * management duties;

  * any other ground duties assigned by Oman Air.



**Duty Period**
    

A period which starts when flight crew or cabin crew members are required by the Company to report for or to commence a duty and ends when that person is free from all duties, including post-flight duty. For simulators, the duty period starts at the beginning of the Pre-briefing and ends at the end of the De-briefing.

**Early Start**
    

Any duty that is commenced in the period 0500-0659 hours local time.

**Fatigue**
    

A physiological state of reduced mental or physical performance capability resulting from sleep loss or extended wakefulness, circadian phase, or workload (mental and/or physical activity) that can impair a crew member's alertness and ability to safely operate an aircraft or perform safety-related duties.

**Flight Crew Member**
    

A licensed crew member charged with duties essential to the operation of an aircraft during a flight duty period such as Pilots.

**Flight Duty Period (FDP)**
    

A period which commences when a crew member is required to report for duty that includes a flight or a series of flights and which finishes when the aeroplane finally comes to rest at the end of the last flight on which he/she is a crew member.

**Flight Time/ Block Hours - aeroplanes**
    

The total time from the moment an aeroplane first moves for the purpose of taking off until the moment it finally comes to rest at the end of the flight.

_"Flight time" as here defined is synonymous with the term "block to block" time or "chock to chock" time in general usage which is measured from the time an aeroplane first moves for the purpose of taking off until it finally stops at the end of the flight._

**FTL variation**
    

Any operation conducted outside the limits of the prescriptive FTL regulation, and under a specific approval by the CAA on the basis of a risk assessment provided by the operator and assessed by the CAA to provide a level of safety equivalent to, or better than, that achieved through the prescriptive fatigue management regulations.

**Home Base**
    

Means the location, assigned by the Company to the crew member, from where the crew member normally starts and ends a duty period or a series of duty periods and where, under normal circumstances, the airline is not responsible for the accommodation of the crew member concerned.

**Late Finish**
    

A duty is a Late Finish when the duty finishes in the period 0100 to 0159 hours local time.

**Local Night**
    

A period of 8 hours falling between 2200 hours and 0800 hours local time.

**Night Duty**
    

A duty period encroaching any portion of the period between 02:00 and 04:59 in the time zone to which the crew is acclimatised.

**Operating Crew Member**
    

A crew member carrying out his/her duties in an aircraft during a flight that is required for the safety of the aeroplane and its occupants, according to their licenses or authorisation.

**Operational Delay**
    

Delays that are beyond the control of the Company such as those that would be caused by weather, aircraft equipment malfunction, and air traffic control delays. **It would not include late arriving passengers, late food service, late fuel trucks, delays in loading baggage-freight-mail, or similar events.**

**Positioning**
    

The practice of transferring crews from place to place as passengers in surface or air transport at the behest of the Company.

**Reporting Time**
    

The time at which a crew member is required by the Company to report for any duty.

**Reserve**
    

Means a period of time during which a crew member is required by the Company to be available to receive an assignment for an FDP, positioning or other duty notified at least 10 hours in advance.

**Rest facility**
    

Means a bunk or seat with leg and foot support suitable for crew member's sleeping on board an aircraft.

**Rest Period**
    

A continuous and defined period of time,subsequent to and/or prior to duty, during which flight or cabin crew members are free of all duties.

**Rostered/Planned Duty**
    

A duty period, or series of duty periods, with stipulated start and finish times, notified by the Company to crews in advance.

**Scheduled Duty**
    

The allocation of a specific flight or flights or other duties to a crew member within the pre-notified rostered/planned series of duty periods.

**Sector**
    

Segment of a Flight Duty Period (FDP) between an aircraft first moving for the purpose of taking off until it comes to rest after landing on the designated parking position.

**Split Duty**
    

A flying duty period, which consists of two or more sectors, separated by less than a minimum rest period.

**Standby Duty**
    

A period during which the Company places constraints on a crew member who would otherwise be off duty. However, it shall not include any time during which the airline requires a crew member to be contactable for the purpose of giving notification of a duty, which is due to start 10 hours or more ahead.

**Suitable Accommodation**
    

A well-furnished bedroom, which is subject to minimum noise, is well ventilated, and has the facility to control the levels of light and temperature.

**Time Difference**
    

The number of hours separating local standard time at two locations (disregarding 'daylight saving time').

**Travelling**
    

All time spent by a crew member transiting between the place of rest, and the place of reporting for duty.

**Unforeseen operational circumstance**
    

An unplanned event, such as un-forecast weather, equipment malfunction, or air traffic delay that is beyond the control of the Company.

**Week**
    

A period of seven consecutive days starting on Sunday at 00:00 UTC.

**Window of Circadian Low ('WOCL')**
    

Means the period between 0200 and 0559 hours in the time zone to which a crew member is acclimatised.


---

## 7.1.2 - Definitions

# Definitions

For the purpose of this chapter (OM-A 7), the following definitions shall apply:

**Acclimatised**
    

When a crew member has spent 3 consecutive local nights on the ground within a local time zone band, which is two hours wide, and is able to take uninterrupted night's sleep. The crew member will remain acclimatised thereafter until a duty period finishes at a place where local time differs by more than 2 hours from that at the point of departure.

**Accommodation (also see - Suitable Accommodation)**
    

For the purpose of standby and split duty, a quiet and comfortable place not open to the public with the ability to control light and temperature, equipped with adequate furniture that provides a crew member with the possibility to sleep, with enough capacity to accommodate all crew members present at the same time and with access to food and drink. For guidance refer [OM-A 7.1.2.1](javascript:void\(0\);).

**Actual Flight Operation**
    

Actual flight operation starts at the reporting time and ends when the crew goes off duty.

**Adequate Facilities**
    

A quiet and comfortable place not open to the public.

**Augmented Flight Crew**
    

A flight crew that comprises more than the minimum number required to operate the aeroplane and in which each flight crew member can leave his or her assigned post and be replaced by another appropriately qualified flight crew member for the purpose of in-flight rest.

**Block Time**
    

The time between the time an aircraft first moves from its parking place for the purpose of taking off until it comes to rest on the designated parking position or until all engines are stopped.

**Break**
    

A period of time within a flight duty period, shorter than a rest period, counting as duty and during which a crew member is free of all tasks.

**Cabin Crew Member**
    

An appropriately qualified crew member, other than a flight crew member, who is assigned by the Company to perform duty related to safety of flight and passengers during operations of aircraft.

**Commander (the pilot in command)**
    

The pilot designated by the Company being in command and charged with the safe conduct of a flight.

**Contactable**
    

A short period of time during the day, other than a day off, during which the Company requires a crew member to be at an agreed location for the purpose of giving notification of a duty period, which will commence not less than 10 hours ahead.

Notification of rostered duties and changes thereof is given to the crew by providing them with published rosters and change information through AIMS. When it is required to otherwise contact the crew to notify them for any change in duties, it shall be ensured that it does not disrupt thier sleep and minimum rest periods. Refer OM-A 7.1.6.9 - Modes of Notification.

**Crew Member**
    

A person assigned by the Company to perform duty on an aircraft.

**Days Off**
    

Periods of relaxation free from all duties. A single day off shall include two local nights (minimum of 34 hours period). Consecutive days off shall include a further local night for each additional consecutive day off. A rest period may be included as part of a day off.

**Delayed Reporting**
    

Means the postponement of a scheduled FDP by the operator before a crew member has left the place of rest.

**Duty**
    

Any task that crew member is required by the Company to perform, including, for example, flight duty, administrative work, training, positioning and standby when it is likely to induce fatigue. Administrative work has to be understood as any task that a crew member is required to carry out associated with the business of the airline (AOC holder). It includes:

  * time spent in the office on official duties;

  * management duties;

  * any other ground duties assigned by Oman Air.



**Duty Period**
    

A period which starts when flight crew or cabin crew members are required by the Company to report for or to commence a duty and ends when that person is free from all duties, including post-flight duty. For simulators, the duty period starts at the beginning of the Pre-briefing and ends at the end of the De-briefing.

**Early Start**
    

Any duty that is commenced in the period 0500-0659 hours local time.

**Fatigue**
    

A physiological state of reduced mental or physical performance capability resulting from sleep loss or extended wakefulness, circadian phase, or workload (mental and/or physical activity) that can impair a crew member's alertness and ability to safely operate an aircraft or perform safety-related duties.

**Flight Crew Member**
    

A licensed crew member charged with duties essential to the operation of an aircraft during a flight duty period such as Pilots.

**Flight Duty Period (FDP)**
    

A period which commences when a crew member is required to report for duty that includes a flight or a series of flights and which finishes when the aeroplane finally comes to rest at the end of the last flight on which he/she is a crew member.

**Flight Time/ Block Hours - aeroplanes**
    

The total time from the moment an aeroplane first moves for the purpose of taking off until the moment it finally comes to rest at the end of the flight.

_"Flight time" as here defined is synonymous with the term "block to block" time or "chock to chock" time in general usage which is measured from the time an aeroplane first moves for the purpose of taking off until it finally stops at the end of the flight._

**FTL variation**
    

Any operation conducted outside the limits of the prescriptive FTL regulation, and under a specific approval by the CAA on the basis of a risk assessment provided by the operator and assessed by the CAA to provide a level of safety equivalent to, or better than, that achieved through the prescriptive fatigue management regulations.

**Home Base**
    

Means the location, assigned by the Company to the crew member, from where the crew member normally starts and ends a duty period or a series of duty periods and where, under normal circumstances, the airline is not responsible for the accommodation of the crew member concerned.

**Late Finish**
    

A duty is a Late Finish when the duty finishes in the period 0100 to 0159 hours local time.

**Local Night**
    

A period of 8 hours falling between 2200 hours and 0800 hours local time.

**Night Duty**
    

A duty period encroaching any portion of the period between 02:00 and 04:59 in the time zone to which the crew is acclimatised.

**Operating Crew Member**
    

A crew member carrying out his/her duties in an aircraft during a flight that is required for the safety of the aeroplane and its occupants, according to their licenses or authorisation.

**Operational Delay**
    

Delays that are beyond the control of the Company such as those that would be caused by weather, aircraft equipment malfunction, and air traffic control delays. **It would not include late arriving passengers, late food service, late fuel trucks, delays in loading baggage-freight-mail, or similar events.**

**Positioning**
    

The practice of transferring crews from place to place as passengers in surface or air transport at the behest of the Company.

**Reporting Time**
    

The time at which a crew member is required by the Company to report for any duty.

**Reserve**
    

Means a period of time during which a crew member is required by the Company to be available to receive an assignment for an FDP, positioning or other duty notified at least 10 hours in advance.

**Rest facility**
    

Means a bunk or seat with leg and foot support suitable for crew member's sleeping on board an aircraft.

**Rest Period**
    

A continuous and defined period of time,subsequent to and/or prior to duty, during which flight or cabin crew members are free of all duties.

**Rostered/Planned Duty**
    

A duty period, or series of duty periods, with stipulated start and finish times, notified by the Company to crews in advance.

**Scheduled Duty**
    

The allocation of a specific flight or flights or other duties to a crew member within the pre-notified rostered/planned series of duty periods.

**Sector**
    

Segment of a Flight Duty Period (FDP) between an aircraft first moving for the purpose of taking off until it comes to rest after landing on the designated parking position.

**Split Duty**
    

A flying duty period, which consists of two or more sectors, separated by less than a minimum rest period.

**Standby Duty**
    

A period during which the Company places constraints on a crew member who would otherwise be off duty. However, it shall not include any time during which the airline requires a crew member to be contactable for the purpose of giving notification of a duty, which is due to start 10 hours or more ahead.

**Suitable Accommodation**
    

A well-furnished bedroom, which is subject to minimum noise, is well ventilated, and has the facility to control the levels of light and temperature.

**Time Difference**
    

The number of hours separating local standard time at two locations (disregarding 'daylight saving time').

**Travelling**
    

All time spent by a crew member transiting between the place of rest, and the place of reporting for duty.

**Unforeseen operational circumstance**
    

An unplanned event, such as un-forecast weather, equipment malfunction, or air traffic delay that is beyond the control of the Company.

**Week**
    

A period of seven consecutive days starting on Sunday at 00:00 UTC.

**Window of Circadian Low ('WOCL')**
    

Means the period between 0200 and 0559 hours in the time zone to which a crew member is acclimatised.


---

## 7.1.2.1 - Guidance-Definitions

# Guidance-Definitions

_**Acclimatised**_

A crew member remains acclimatised to the local time of his reference time during 47 hours 59 minutes after reporting no matter how many time zones he has crossed.

As soon as forty-eight (48) hours have elapsed, the state of acclimatisation is derived from the time elapsed since reporting at reference time and the number of time zones crossed.

A crew member is considered to be in an unknown state of acclimatisation after the first 48 hours of the rotation have elapsed unless he remains in the first arrival destination time zone (either for rest or any duties) in accordance with Table hereunder:

**Time difference (h) between reference time and local time where the crew member starts the next duty** | **Time elapsed since reporting at reference time**  
---|---  
**< 48** | **48 - 71:59** | **72 - 95:59** | **96 - 119:59** | **≥ 120**  
**< 4** | B | D | D | D | D  
**≤ 6** | B | X | D | D | D  
**≤ 9** | B | X | X | D | D  
**≤ 12** | B | X | X | X | D  
  
Note:

'reference time' means the local time at the reporting point situated in a 2-hour wide time zone band around the local time where a crew member is acclimatised.

'B' means acclimatised to the local time of the departure time zone,

'D' means acclimatised to the local time where the crew member starts his/her next duty, and

'X' means that a crew member is in an unknown state of acclimatisation.

Should a crew member's rotation include additional duties that end in a different time zone than his or her first arrival destination's time zone while he is considered to be in an unknown state of acclimatisation, then the crew member remains in an unknown state of acclimatisation until he or she:

  1. has taken the rest period required at home base;

  2. has taken the rest period required at the new location; or

  3. has been undertaking duties starting at and returning to the time zone of the new location until he or she becomes acclimatised in accordance with the values in Table above.




To determine the state of acclimatisation, the following criteria should be applied:

  1. the greater of the time differences between the time zone where he was last acclimatised or the local time of his last departure point and the new location; and

  2. the time elapsed since reporting at home base for the first time during the rotation.




_**Adequate Furniture For 'Accommodation'**_

Adequate furniture for crew member accommodation should include a seat that reclines at least 45 degree back angle to the vertical, has a seat width of at least 20 inches (50 cm) and provides leg and foot support.

_**Determination of Disruptive Schedules**_

If a crew member is acclimatised to the local time at his/her home base, the local time at the home base should be used to consider an FDP as 'disruptive schedule'. This applies to operations within the 2-hour wide time zone surrounding the local time at the home base, if a crew member is acclimatised to the local time at his/her home base. Schedule may be disruptive due to early starts, late finishes or night duties.

_**Operating Crew Member**_

A person on board an aircraft is either a crew member or a passenger. If a crew member is not a passenger on board an aircraft he should be considered as 'carrying out duties'. The crew member remains an operating crew member during in-flight rest. In-flight rest counts in full as FDP, and for the purpose of [OM-A 7.1.4](javascript:void\(0\);).


---

## 7.1.3 - Home Base

# Home Base

The home base is a single airport location assigned with a high degree of permanence.

Oman Air crew members home base is Muscat International Airport.

In the case of a change of home base, the first recurrent extended recovery rest period (Days Off) prior to starting duty at the new home base is increased to seventy (72) hours, including three (3) local nights. Travelling time between the former home base and the new home base is positioning.

_**Travelling Time**_

Crew members are advised to consider making arrangements for temporary accommodation closer to their home base if the travelling time from their residence to their home base usually exceeds ninety (90) minutes.


---

## 7.1.4 - Limits for Flying Time and Duty Period

# Limits for Flying Time and Duty Period

No person shall act as an operating crew member of the flight crew of an aircraft if at the beginning of the flight the aggregate of all his previous flight times;

  1. During the period of twenty-eight (28) consecutive days expiring at the end of the day on which the flight begins exceeds one hundred (100) block hours; or

  2. During the period of twelve months (12) expiring at the end of the previous month exceeds nine hundred (900) block hours.




The maximum cumulative duty hours for flight crew of an aeroplane shall not exceed;

  1. Fifty-five (55) hours in any seven (7) consecutive days, but may be increased to sixty (60) hours, when rostered duty covering a series of duty periods, once commenced, is subject to unforeseen delays;

  2. Ninety-five (95) hours in any fourteen (14) consecutive days; and

  3. One hundred and ninety (190) hours in any twenty-eight (28) consecutive days.





---

## 7.1.5 - Duty Cycle and Day Off

# Duty Cycle and Day Off

A Crew Member:

  1. Shall not be on duty more than seven (7) consecutive days between days off, but may be positioned, and may operate only, under unforeseen circumstances, to the usual operating base on the eighth day, provided they are then allocated a minimum of two (2) consecutive days off, and

  2. Shall have two (2) consecutive days off in any consecutive fourteen (14) days following the previous two (2) consecutive days off, and

  3. Shall have a minimum of seven (7) days off in any consecutive twenty-eight (28) days, and

  4. Shall have an average of at least eight (8) days off in each consecutive twenty-eight (28) day period, averaged over three (3) such periods.





---

## 7.1.6 - Flight Time Limitations (FTL) and Flight D

# Late Finish/Early Starts/Night Duties

The conditions set herein (OM-A 7.1.6.1 and OM-A 7.1.6.1.1) only apply when a crew member is acclimatised. Sleep deprivation, leading to the onset of fatigue, can arise if an operating crew member is required to report early for duty on a number of consecutive days. Therefore:

  1. Not more than three (3) consecutive duties that occur in any part of the period 0100 to 0659 hours local time can be undertaken, nor will there be more than four (4) such duties in any seven (7) consecutive days.

  2. Any run of consecutive duties (Late Finishes or Night Duties or Early Starts) can only be broken by a period of not less than thirty-four (34) consecutive hours free from such duties. This thirty-four (34) consecutive hours may include a duty that is not an Early, Late or Night duty.

  3. Any duties scheduled to be conducted within any part of the period 0200 to 0459 hours local time, for a minimum of two (2) and a maximum of three (3) consecutive nights, then operating crew members shall finish the duty preceding this series of duties by 2100 hours local time before covering the block of consecutive night duties (this allows operating crew members a rest period during a local night).





---

## 7.1.6.1 - Late Finish/Early Starts/Night Duties

# Late Finish/Early Starts/Night Duties

The conditions set herein (OM-A 7.1.6.1 and OM-A 7.1.6.1.1) only apply when a crew member is acclimatised. Sleep deprivation, leading to the onset of fatigue, can arise if an operating crew member is required to report early for duty on a number of consecutive days. Therefore:

  1. Not more than three (3) consecutive duties that occur in any part of the period 0100 to 0659 hours local time can be undertaken, nor will there be more than four (4) such duties in any seven (7) consecutive days.

  2. Any run of consecutive duties (Late Finishes or Night Duties or Early Starts) can only be broken by a period of not less than thirty-four (34) consecutive hours free from such duties. This thirty-four (34) consecutive hours may include a duty that is not an Early, Late or Night duty.

  3. Any duties scheduled to be conducted within any part of the period 0200 to 0459 hours local time, for a minimum of two (2) and a maximum of three (3) consecutive nights, then operating crew members shall finish the duty preceding this series of duties by 2100 hours local time before covering the block of consecutive night duties (this allows operating crew members a rest period during a local night).





---

## 7.1.6.1 - Late Finish/Early Starts/Night Duties

# Late Finish/Early Starts/Night Duties

The conditions set herein (OM-A 7.1.6.1 and OM-A 7.1.6.1.1) only apply when a crew member is acclimatised. Sleep deprivation, leading to the onset of fatigue, can arise if an operating crew member is required to report early for duty on a number of consecutive days. Therefore:

  1. Not more than three (3) consecutive duties that occur in any part of the period 0100 to 0659 hours local time can be undertaken, nor will there be more than four (4) such duties in any seven (7) consecutive days.

  2. Any run of consecutive duties (Late Finishes or Night Duties or Early Starts) can only be broken by a period of not less than thirty-four (34) consecutive hours free from such duties. This thirty-four (34) consecutive hours may include a duty that is not an Early, Late or Night duty.

  3. Any duties scheduled to be conducted within any part of the period 0200 to 0459 hours local time, for a minimum of two (2) and a maximum of three (3) consecutive nights, then operating crew members shall finish the duty preceding this series of duties by 2100 hours local time before covering the block of consecutive night duties (this allows operating crew members a rest period during a local night).





---

## 7.1.6.1.1 - Night Duties

# Night Duties

When establishing the maximum FDP for consecutive night duties, the number of sectors is limited to four (4) sectors per duty.

Risk management shall be applied to actively manage the fatiguing night duties of more than ten (10) hours (referred to below as 'long night duties') in relation to the surrounding duties and rest periods.

When rostering long night duties, it is critical for the crew member to obtain sufficient sleep before such duties when he is adapted to being awake during day time hours at the local time where he is acclimatised. To optimise alertness on long night duties, the likelihood of obtaining sleep as close as possible to the start of the FDP should be considered, when rostering rest periods before long night duties, by providing sufficient time to the crew member to adapt to being awake during the night. Rostering practices leading to extended wakefulness before reporting for such duties should be avoided. Principles to be applied to the rostering of long night duties include:

  1. avoiding long night duties after extended recovery rest periods (Days Off);

  2. progressively delaying the rostered ending time of the FDPs preceding long night duties;

  3. starting a block of night duties with a shorter FDP; and

  4. avoiding the sequence of early starts and long night duties.





---

## 7.1.6.2 - Extentions of Flying Duty Period by In-f

# Extentions of Flying Duty Period by In-flight Relief

When any additional operating crew member is carried to provide in-flight relief with the intent of extending an FDP, that individual shall hold qualifications which are equal or superior to those held by the crew member who is to be rested. To take advantage of this facility the division of duty and rest between crew members must be kept in balance. It is unnecessary for the relieving crew member to rest in between the times relief is provided for other crew members.

When in-flight relief is utilized, there must be for the crew members resting, a comfortable reclining seat provided with foot, legs and arm rests, or a crew rest compartment with a bunk or equivalent flat-bed first or business class seat, separated by at least a side seat (where possible) from any passenger and screened from the flight deck.

As a contingency procedure in case of unforeseen circumstances that make the accepted in-flight rest facility unserviceable, an equivalent rest facility according to the configuration/ layout of the passenger cabin has to be assigned to the affected crew member.

An inflight rest period is only applied during cruise, not during the take-off or landing phases of a flight.

  * Take-off phase is considered to be the first 45 minutes of a flight.

  * Landing phase is considered to the the last 45 minutes of a flight.




An inflight rest period for each flight crew member must allow for 90 minutes of rest.

The inflight rest periods must allow for the impact of 'sleep inertia', at least 15 minutes must be given before recommencing safety-related duties.

An inflight rest period for the flight crew members performing the aircraft landing on the last flight in the FDP must allow for two(2) hours of continous inflight rest.

A total in-flight rest of less than three (3) hours does not allow for the extension of an FDP, but where the total in-flight rest, which need not be consecutive, is three (3) hours or more, then the permitted FDP may be extended as follows:

  * If rest is taken in a bunk or equivalent flatbed seat (see note 1 below): A period equal to one half of the total rest taken, provided that the maximum FDP permissible shall be eighteen (18) hours or nineteen (19) hours in the case of cabin crew member.

  * If rest is taken in a seat (see note 2 below): A period equal to one third of the total rest taken, provided that the maximum FDP permissible shall be fifteen (15) hours or sixteen (16) hours in the case of cabin crew member.




_**Note 1:** 'Equivalent flatbed' means a business class or first class seat that allows for a flat or near flat sleeping position. It reclines to at least 80 degrees back angle to the vertical. Separated by at least a side seat (where possible) from any passenger and screened from the flight deck._

_**Note 2:** 'Seat' means a business class or first class seat that reclines at least 40 degrees from the vertical, provides leg and foot support. Separated by at least a side seat (where possible) from any passenger and screened from the flight deck._


---

## 7.1.6.3 - Split Duty

# Split Duty

When an FDP consists of two or more sectors/duties, of which one can be a positioning journey counted as a sector, but separated by less than a minimum rest period, then the FDP may be extended beyond that permitted by the amounts indicated below:

**Consecutive Hours Rest** | **Maximum Extension of FDP**  
---|---  
Less than 3 hours | Nil  
3 to 10 hours | A period equal to half of the consecutive hours taken.  
  
The break on the ground within the FDP has a minimum of three (3) consecutive hours.

The rest period shall not include the time allowed for immediate post-flight duties, pre-flight duties and travelling.

Unless otherwise specified, the minimum total time for post and pre-flight duties shall be as per [OM-A 7.1.10](javascript:void\(0\);).

Unless otherwise established, a standard time of (30) minutes shall be included as the time allowed for travelling. This only applies when the rest facilities are not located within the Airport and external transport is required to drop and pick the crew from the rest facility.

When the break is six (6) hours or less, it will suffice if a quiet and comfortable place, not open to the public, is provided with comfortable reclining seats (reclining more than forty [40] degrees and provide foot and leg rest), or bunks are available. **Any time of the actual break exceeding six (6) hours or any time of the break that encroaches the WOCL does not count for the extension of the FDP.**

If rest is taken in the aircraft on the ground, the crew must have adequate control of the temperature and ventilation within the aircraft, either by use of a ground power unit or the aircraft internal power units. The passengers must not be on board. In such cases, 30 minutes time is considered for immediate post-flight duties and pre-flight duties.

If the break is more than six (6) consecutive hours, or if the break encroaches the window of circadian low (WOCL), then suitable accommodation must be provided.

Split duty cannot be combined with in-flight rest.


---

## 7.1.6.4 - Rest Period

# Rest Period

All crew members must be notified in good time of a flying duty period so that sufficient and uninterrupted pre-flight rest can be obtained.

When away from base, the crew shall be provided with the opportunity and the facilities for adequate pre-flight rest. Suitable accommodation must be provided. When flights are carried out at such short notice that it is impracticable to arrange suitable accommodation, then this responsibility devolves to the aircraft commander.

The minimum rest period which must be provided before undertaking a flying duty period shall be:

  1. at least as long as the preceding duty period; or

  2. twelve (12) hours;

  3. whichever is the greater.




Refer to [OM-A 7.2.2](javascript:void\(0\);) for conditions under which rest may be reduced.

If the preceding duty period, which includes any time spent on positioning, exceeded eighteen (18) hours, then the ensuing rest period must include a local night.

**Minimum Rest Away from Base:** The time considered for physiological needs is 1 hour. Consequently, if the travelling time to the suitable accommodation is more than 30 minutes, the rest period should be increased by twice the amount of difference of travelling time above 30 minutes.

**Minimum Rest Period at Home Base if Suitable Accommodation is Provided:** The minimum rest period away from home may be applied during a rotation which includes a rest period at a crew member's home base, if the crew member does not rest at his/her residence, or temporary accommodation because suitable accomodation is provided by the airline. This type of roster is know as "back-to-back operation".

After being called out from a standby duty the length of minimum rest shall be determined by the length of standby duty, plus any time spent on positioning, and any FDP completed.

Crew members who inform the airline that they are having difficulty in achieving adequate pre-flight rest must be given the opportunity to consult an aviation medical specialist.


---

## 7.1.6.5 - Mixed Duties

# Mixed Duties

When a crew member is required to report for duty in advance of the stipulated report time for a scheduled flight, to carry out a task at the behalf of the company, then the time spent on that task shall be part of the subsequent FDP.

**_Administrative Duties_**

For crew members that serve in a management function, the administrative or office time is considered part of the flight duty period (FDP) when this time immediately precedes (i.e.without an intervening rest period) a FDP.

_**Training Period**_

Training period(s) prior to a flight are considered part of flight duty period when this time immediately precedes (i.e. without an intervening rest periods) a flight duty period in which that person participates as a crew member.

**_Mixed Simulator and Aircraft Flying_**

When a fight crew member flies in the simulator, either on a check or training flight, or as a Training Captain or Instructor, and then within the same duty period flies as a flight crew member on a scheduled commercial transport flight, all the time spent in the simulator is counted in full towards the subsequent FDP. Simulator flying does not count as a sector, but the FDP allowable is calculated from the report time of the simulator detail.


---

## 7.1.6.6 - Travelling Time

# Travelling Time

Travelling time, other than that time spent on positioning, does not count as duty.

When crew members are required to travel from their home to an aerodrome other than the one from which they normally operate, any travelling time over and above the journey time from home to the usual operating aerodrome shall be classed as positioning. Notional times for any additional travelling shall be agreed with the CAA.


---

## 7.1.6.7 - Positioning

# Positioning

All operating crew member's time spent positioning for company operations shall count as Duty Time, but positioning does not count as a sector when calculating the FDP. In these circumstances the FDP commences not later than the time at which the operating crew member reports for the positioning journey, or positions in accordance with [OM-A 7.1.6.6](javascript:void\(0\);) above.

If, after a positioning journey, the operating crew member spends less than a minimum rest period at suitable accommodation provided by the company, and then carries out an FDP, the positioning will be counted as a sector if a split duty is claimed when calculating the allowable FDP. If it is not, a split duty FDP will not be used.


---

## 7.1.6.8 - Delayed Reporting Time in a Single FDP

# Delayed Reporting Time in a Single FDP

When an operating crew member is informed of a delay to the reporting time due to a changed schedule, before leaving the place of rest, the FDP shall be calculated as follow:

  1. When the delay is less than four (4) hours, the maximum FDP allowed shall be based on the original report time and the FDP shall start at the actual report time;

  2. Where the delay is four (4) hours or more, the maximum FDP shall be based on the more limiting time band of the planned and the actual report time and the FDP starts four (4) hours after the original report time.




When the crew member is informed before leaving the place of rest of a delay in reporting time of ten (10) hours or more ahead, and that crew member is not further disturbed by the airline until a mutually agreed hour, then that elapsed time is classed as a rest period. If, upon the resumption of duty, further delays occur, then the appropriate criteria in this para and para above shall be applied to the rearranged reporting time.

**_Modes of Notification_**

**Notification Medium** | **Time Scope**  
---|---  
Thru the rostering system | Always  
Text message(via SMS) | Always for affected duties within 24 hours of commencement  
Telephone call (when crew is on layover, telephone call should be via hotel room) | Within 3 hours of departure time or commencement of airport standby. For urgent situation (see note 1), provided crew member is not on mandatoy rest period.  
Email to crew member's WY email account | As required  
Email, notice letter via Airport Service Manage/hotel reception desk | As required, during stay at outstation hotel.  
  
_**Note 1:** Urgent situation means on the day of operation; crew member is required to report for earlier duty._

During layover, when dealing with the group, only the following crew members should be contacted via hotel room phone; Commander, First Officer and Cabin Director (or the designated Cabin Director), or the designated crew member left behind at the hotel (when other crew members leave the hotel and are not reachable).

When possible, interference with the crew member's sleeping patterns should be avoided.


---

## 7.1.6.9 - Maximum FDP

# Maximum FDP

Standard reporting times are given in [OM-A 7.1.10](javascript:void\(0\);). Pre-flight duties are part of the FDP; a minimum of thirty (30) minutes duty will be allowed for post-flight activities. The time spent between reporting for a flight and the completion of post-flight tasks determines the length of the subsequent rest period. If this "period" for post FDP duties is routinely exceeded, then the post FDP duty period stated in the scheme must be revised to better represent the actual time taken.

h-hours; m-minutes

A non-standard reporting time designed to take advantage of an increased FDP from a more favourable time band, must not be used.

Table A applies when the FDP starts at a place where the crew member is acclimatised; Table B applies at other times:

Table 7.1.6.9-1 **Table A - Two or more Flight Crew - Acclimatised****Local Time of Start** | **Sectors**  
---|---  
**1** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** | **6** | **7** | **8 or more**  
06:00-07:59 | 13h | 12h 15m | 11h 30m | 10h 45m | 10h | 09h 30m | 09h | 09h  
08:00-12:59 | 14h | 13h 15m | 12h 30m | 11h 45m | 11h | 10h 30m | 10h | 09h30m  
13:00-17:59 | 13h | 12h 15m | 11h 30m | 10h 45m | 10h | 09h 30m | 09h | 09h  
18:00-21:59 | 12h | 11h 15m | 10h 30m | 09h 45m | 09h | 09h | 09h | 09h  
22:00-05:59 | 11h | 10h 15m | 09h 30m | 09h | 09h | 09h | 09h | 09h  
  
Table 7.1.6.9-2 **Table B - Two or more Flight Crew - Not Acclimatised****Length of preceding rest (hours)** | **Sectors**  
---|---  
**1** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** | **6** | **7 or more**  
Up to 18 or over 30 | 13h | 12h 15m | 1h 30m | 10h 45m | 10h | 09h 15m | 09h  
Between 18 and 30 | 11h 30m | 11h | 10h 30m | 09h 45m | 09h | 09h | 09h  
  
Note:

The practice of inserting a short duty into a rest period of between 18 and 30 hours, in order to produce a rest period of less than 18 hours, thereby taking advantage of the longer FDP contained in Table B, is not permitted.


---

## 7.1.6.10 - Limits on Two Flight Crew

# Limits on Two Flight Crew

When an aeroplane flight crew is only two pilots, the allowable FDP shall be calculated as follows. A sector scheduled for more than seven (7) hours is considered as a multi-sector flight, as below: (not applicable to cabin crew)

Table 7.1.6.10-1 **Table C- Two or more Flight Crew - Flight Time greater than 7 hrs** Scheduled Sector Length / Times | Acclimatised | Not Acclimatised  
---|---|---  
Sector length over 7 hours but not more than 9 hours | 2 sectors | 4 sectors  
Sector length over 9 hours but not more than 11 hours | 3 sectors | 4 sectors  
Sector length over 11 hours | 4 sectors | Not applicable  
  
The appropriate Table (A or B) is then entered with the start time of the duty period and the 'factored' number of sectors, to determine the allowable FDP.

When an additional, current, type rated pilot is an operating crew member, then these limits do not apply and the permissible FDP is determined by entering Table A or B with time of start and the actual sectors planned.


---

## 7.1.7 - Standby and Duties at the Airport

# Standby and Duties at the Airport

If crew members are assigned to standby or to any duty at the airport, the following shall apply:

  1. Standby and any duty at the airport shall be in the roster and the start and end time of standby shall be defined and notified in advance to the crew members concerned to provide them with the opportunity to plan adequate rest;

  2. A crew member is considered on airport standby from reporting at the reporting point until the end of the notified airport standby period;

  3. Airport standby shall count in full as duty period for the purpose of [OM-A 7.1.6](javascript:void\(0\););

  4. If an assigned FDP starts during airport standby, the following applies:

     1. the FDP counts from the start of FDP. The maximum FDP is reduced by anytime spent on standby in excess of four (4) hours;

     2. the maximum combined duration of airport standby and assigned FDP is sixteen (16) hours.

  5. Any duty at the airport shall count in full as duty period and the FDP shall count in full from the airport duty reporting time;

  6. Accomodation shall be provided to the crew member on airport standby which shall include the following:

     1. The resting area at the airport shall be separated from the operational area of crew activities by a sound-proof door;

     2. Suitable secure baggage storage facilities shall be provided within this rest area;

     3. Suitable seating that allows to recline more than 45 degree back angle to the vertical; and provides leg and foot support;

     4. It is separated from other seating by at least a curtain to provide darkness and some sound mitigation, and is reasonably free from disturbance by other crew members.

  7. If not leading to the assignment of an FDP, airport standby shall be followed by a rest period as specified in [OM-A 7.1.6.4](javascript:void\(0\);)

  8. Standby other than airport standby:

     1. The maximum duration of standby other than airport standby is sixteen (16) hours;

     2. It shall be ensured that the combination of standby and FDP do not lead to more than eighteen (18) hours awake time;

     3. 25 percent of time spent on standby other than airport standby counts as duty time for the purpose of [OM-A 7.1.4](javascript:void\(0\););

     4. Standby is followed by a rest period in accordance with [OM-A 7.1.6.4](javascript:void\(0\);) / [OM-A 7.1.9](javascript:void\(0\););

     5. Standby ceases when the crew member reports at the designated reporting point;

     6. If standby ceases within the first six (6) hours, the maximum FDP counts from reporting;

     7. If standby ceases after the first six (6) hours, the maximum FDP is reduced by the amount of standby time exceeding six (6) hours;
