     8. If the FDP is extended due to in-flight rest according to [OM-A 7.1.6.2](javascript:void\(0\);), or to split duty according to [OM-A 7.1.6.3](javascript:void\(0\);), the six (6) hours of paragraphs (f.) and (g.) are extended to eight (8) hours;

     9. If standby starts between 23:00 and 07:00, the time between 23:00 and 07:00 does not count towards the reduction of the FDP under paragraphs (f.), (g.) and (h.) until the crew member is contacted by the airline; and

     10. Response Time: Crew on standby are expected to report within 01 hour from the time of notification.

  9. Minimum Rest and Standby

     1. If airport or other standby initially assigned is reduced by the airline during standby that does not lead to an assignment to a flight duty period, the minimum rest requirements specified in [OM-A 7.1.6.4](javascript:void\(0\);) / [OM-A 7.1.9](javascript:void\(0\);) shall apply.

     2. If a minimum rest period as specified in [OM-A 7.1.6.4](javascript:void\(0\);) / [OM-A 7.1.9](javascript:void\(0\);) is provided before reporting for the duty assigned during the standby, this time period shall not count as standby duty.

     3. Standby other than airport standby counts (partly) as duty for the purpose of [OMA 7.1.4](javascript:void\(0\);) only. If a crew member receives an assignment during standby other than airport standby, the actual reporting time at the designated reporting point shall be used for the purpose of [OM-A 7.1.6.4](javascript:void\(0\);) / [OM-A 7.1.9](javascript:void\(0\);).

  10. Notification of assigned duties during standby other than airport standby shall avoid interference with sleeping patterns.

  11. Awake Time:Scientific research shows that continuous awake in excess of 18 hours can reduce the alertness and shall be avoided.





---

## 7.1.8 - Reserve Duty

# Reserve Duty

Currently, crew members are not assigned reserve duty.


---

## 7.1.9 - Cabin Crew Requirements

# Cabin Crew Requirements

The limitations, which shall be applied to cabin crew, are those applicable to flight crew members, but with the following differences:

  1. An FDP can be one (1) hour longer than that permitted for flight crew. The FDP and limits set on early starts for cabin crew shall be based on the time at which the flight crew report for their FDP. Cabin crew FDP shall start at the report time of the cabin crew.

  2. The minimum rest period which will be provided before undertaking a flying duty period shall be:

     1. at least as long as the preceding duty period less one (1) hour; or

     2. eleven (11) hours; whichever is the greater.

  3. The combined sum of standby time and subsequent FDP can be one (1) hour longer than that permitted for flight crew.

  4. The maximum duty hours for cabin crew shall not exceed;

     1. Sixty (60) hours in any seven (7) consecutive days, but may be increased to sixty-five (65) hours, when a rostered duty covering a series of duty periods, once commenced, is subject to unforeseen delays;

     2. One hundred and five (105) hours in any fourteen (14) consecutive days; and

     3. Two hundred and ten (210) hours in any twenty-eight (28) consecutive days.

  5. No cabin crew member shall act as an operating crew member of an aircraft if at the beginning of the flight the aggregate of all his/her previous flight times;

     1. during the period of twenty-eight (28) consecutive days expiring at the end of the day on which the flight begins exceeds one hundred (100) Block Hours; or

     2. during the period of twelve (12) consecutive months expiring at the end of the previous month exceeds one thousand (1000) Block hours.





---

## 7.1.10 - Reporting Time

# Reporting Time

Oman Air - Reporting Time

Time before scheduled departure/start time (h-hour/ m-minutes)

Table 7.1.10-1 **Reporting Time** | **A330** | **B787** | **B737**  
---|---|---|---  
**_AT BASE_**  
Flight Crew-Flight Duty | 1h 15m | 1h 15m | 1h 15m  
Flight Crew-Simulator Duty | 2h | 2h | 2h  
Cabin Crew-Flight Duty | 1h 30m | 1h 30m | 1h 15m  
Flight Crew/Cabin Crew-Positioning (Deadheading) | 1h | 1h | 1h  
**_OUTSTATIONS_**  
Flight Crew/Cabin Crew | 1h | 1h | 1h  
  
Note:

All crew are provided with at least 30 minutes at the end of flight duty for post flight duties.

All flight crew are given one hour for de-briefing after simulator session.

Post-flight duty times, shall be determined taking into account the type of operation, the size and type of aircraft and the airport conditions.


---

## 7.1.11 - Records to be Maintained

# Records to be Maintained

Refer [OM-A 2.1.3](javascript:void\(0\);).


---

## 7.2 - Exceedances of Flight and Duty Time Limitati

# Exceedances of Flight and Duty Time Limitations and/or Reductions of Rest Periods

**_Unforeseen Circumstances in Actual Flight Operations - Commander's Discretion_**

The exercise of commander's discretion shall be avoided at home base and/or company hubs where standby or reserve crew member are available.

Crew Planning and Control section will access on a regular basis the series of pairing where commander's discretions has been exercised in order to be aware of possible inconsistencies in rostering.

Exercise of commander's discretion to extend the FDP or reduce rest shall take consideration of factors that may decrease a crew member's alertness levels, such as:

  1. WOCL encroachment

  2. Weather conditions

  3. Complexity of the operation and/or airport environment

  4. Aeroplane malfunctions or specifications

  5. Flight with training or supervisory duties

  6. Increased number of sectors;

  7. Circadian disruption; and

  8. Individual conditions of affected crew members (time since awake, sleep-related factor, workload, etc.)





---

## 7.2 - Exceedances of Flight and Duty Time Limitati

# Exceedances of Flight and Duty Time Limitations and/or Reductions of Rest Periods

**_Unforeseen Circumstances in Actual Flight Operations - Commander's Discretion_**

The exercise of commander's discretion shall be avoided at home base and/or company hubs where standby or reserve crew member are available.

Crew Planning and Control section will access on a regular basis the series of pairing where commander's discretions has been exercised in order to be aware of possible inconsistencies in rostering.

Exercise of commander's discretion to extend the FDP or reduce rest shall take consideration of factors that may decrease a crew member's alertness levels, such as:

  1. WOCL encroachment

  2. Weather conditions

  3. Complexity of the operation and/or airport environment

  4. Aeroplane malfunctions or specifications

  5. Flight with training or supervisory duties

  6. Increased number of sectors;

  7. Circadian disruption; and

  8. Individual conditions of affected crew members (time since awake, sleep-related factor, workload, etc.)





---

## 7.2.1 - Commanders Discretion to Extend a Flying D

# Commanders Discretion to Extend a Flying Duty Period

The commander may, at his discretion, and after taking note of the circumstances of other members of the crew, extend an FDP beyond that permitted in OM-A 7.1.6, provided he is satisfied that the flight can be made safely. The extension shall be calculated according to what actually happens, not on what was planned to happen. An extension of three (3) hours is the maximum permitted, except in cases of emergency.

Note:

In respect of an extension of a flying duty period, an emergency is a situation which in the judgement of the commander presents a serious risk to the health or safety of crew and passengers, or endangers the lives of others (includes security reasons). Discretion reports may be used by the CAA to assess the realism of particular schedules.

The maximum allowable FDP should be determined by the planned block times and factored numbers of sectors as applicable. The factored number of sectors should be increased by the additional number of sectors in case of diversion.

In a Flying Duty Period involving two (2) or more sectors up to a maximum of two (2) hours discretion may be exercised prior to the first and subsequent sectors. On a single sector flight and immediately prior to the last sector on a multi-sector flight, a commander may utilise the full amount of discretion.

A commander may exercise discretion to extend an FDP following a reduced rest period, only exceptionally, and then only to the extent necessary to allow for unforeseen circumstances that become apparent during the last sector.


---

## 7.2.2 - Reduction of Rest

# Reduction of Rest

When away from base, in the case when the rest period earned by a crew member is twelve (12) hours, and suitable accommodation is provided by the airline, then that rest period may be reduced by one (1) hour. In such circumstances, if the travelling time between the aerodrome and the accommodation is more than thirty (30) minutes each way then the rest period must be increased by the amount the total time spent travelling exceeding one hour. The room allocated to the crew member must be available for occupation for a minimum of ten (10) hours. This sub-paragraph does not apply to rest periods that exceed twelve (12) hours.

Exceptionally at home base, individual crew members may be asked to exercise their discretion to reduce rest by up to a maximum of one (1) hour but only to a minimum of twelve (12) hours for flight crew and eleven (11) hours for cabin crew. If discretion is used, it is the responsibility of the airline and the crew member to inform the commander of the flight immediately following the rest period, that a reduced rest period has been taken.

The rest period following a sequence of reduced rest and then an extended FDP, cannot be reduced.


---

## 7.2.3 - Reporting

# Reporting

Whenever a commander extends an FDP or rest is reduced, it shall be reported on the appropriate Discretion Report Form.

If the extension is greater than two (2) hours, or when exercised after any reduced rest period, then the commander's written report, together with the VPFO's comments shall be submitted to the CAA, within fourteen (14) days of the aircraft's return to base.

**_Commanders Discretion Report - Extension of Flying Duty Period/Flying Hours_**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**_Commanders Discretion Report - Reduction of Rest_**![](data:image/png;base64,)


---

## 7.3 - Fatigue Risk Management

# Philosophy and Principles

Fatigue is defined as a physiological state of reduced mental or physical performance capability resulting from sleep loss, extended wakefulness, circadian phase, and/or workload (mental and/or physical activity) that can impair a person’s alertness and ability to perform safety-related operational duties.

Fatigue management refers to the methods by which safety implications of fatigue are addressed.

_**Methods of managing fatigue**_

Fatigue management methods are based on scientific principles and knowledge and operational experience that take into account:

  * the need for adequate sleep (not just resting while awake) to restore and maintain all aspects of waking function (including alertness, physical and mental performance, and mood);

  * the circadian rhythms that drive changes in the ability to perform mental and physical work, and in sleep propensity (the ability to fall asleep and stay asleep), across the 24-hour day;

  * interactions between fatigue and workload in their effects on physical and mental performance; and

  * the operational context and the safety risk that a fatigue-impaired individual represents in that context.




There are two distinct methods for managing fatigue:

  1. A prescriptive approach that requires compliance with duty time limits defined by the regulator (CAA Oman), while identifying and managing fatigue hazards through the safety management system (SMS) processes, and

  2. A performance-based approach that allows an option to develop and implement a fatigue risk management system (FRMS). **Oman Air has not implemented Fatigue Risk Management System (FRMS).**




**Oman Air has established flight time, flight duty period, duty period limitations and rest period requirements that are within the prescription fatigue management regulations as per CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.**

_**Responsibilities**_

The regulator (CAA) provides a regulatory framework that enables fatigue management for managing fatigue-related risks to achieve an acceptable level of safety performance. The CAA has established prescriptive limitation regulations for flight and cabin crew given in CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.

Oman Air (the operator) is responsible for providing fatigue-management education, implementing work schedules in compliance with the CAA regulations that enable crew members to perform their duties safely, and having processes for monitoring and managing fatigue hazards.

Crew members are responsible for arriving fit for duty, including making appropriate use of non-work periods to obtain sleep, and for reporting fatigue hazards.

**_Fatigue Risk Management (FRM)_**

Fatigue risk management is defined as the management of fatigue risk under the prescriptive approach while identifying and managing fatigue hazards through the safety management system (SMS) processes.

**_General Principles_**

Refer to [OM-A 7.1.1](javascript:void\(0\);) for general requirements and principles for fatigue management as per CAR-OPS 1 subpart Q.


---

## 7.3.1 - Philosophy and Principles

# Philosophy and Principles

Fatigue is defined as a physiological state of reduced mental or physical performance capability resulting from sleep loss, extended wakefulness, circadian phase, and/or workload (mental and/or physical activity) that can impair a person’s alertness and ability to perform safety-related operational duties.

Fatigue management refers to the methods by which safety implications of fatigue are addressed.

_**Methods of managing fatigue**_

Fatigue management methods are based on scientific principles and knowledge and operational experience that take into account:

  * the need for adequate sleep (not just resting while awake) to restore and maintain all aspects of waking function (including alertness, physical and mental performance, and mood);

  * the circadian rhythms that drive changes in the ability to perform mental and physical work, and in sleep propensity (the ability to fall asleep and stay asleep), across the 24-hour day;

  * interactions between fatigue and workload in their effects on physical and mental performance; and

  * the operational context and the safety risk that a fatigue-impaired individual represents in that context.




There are two distinct methods for managing fatigue:

  1. A prescriptive approach that requires compliance with duty time limits defined by the regulator (CAA Oman), while identifying and managing fatigue hazards through the safety management system (SMS) processes, and

  2. A performance-based approach that allows an option to develop and implement a fatigue risk management system (FRMS). **Oman Air has not implemented Fatigue Risk Management System (FRMS).**




**Oman Air has established flight time, flight duty period, duty period limitations and rest period requirements that are within the prescription fatigue management regulations as per CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.**

_**Responsibilities**_

The regulator (CAA) provides a regulatory framework that enables fatigue management for managing fatigue-related risks to achieve an acceptable level of safety performance. The CAA has established prescriptive limitation regulations for flight and cabin crew given in CAR-OPS 1 Subpart Q.

Oman Air (the operator) is responsible for providing fatigue-management education, implementing work schedules in compliance with the CAA regulations that enable crew members to perform their duties safely, and having processes for monitoring and managing fatigue hazards.

Crew members are responsible for arriving fit for duty, including making appropriate use of non-work periods to obtain sleep, and for reporting fatigue hazards.

**_Fatigue Risk Management (FRM)_**

Fatigue risk management is defined as the management of fatigue risk under the prescriptive approach while identifying and managing fatigue hazards through the safety management system (SMS) processes.

**_General Principles_**

Refer to [OM-A 7.1.1](javascript:void\(0\);) for general requirements and principles for fatigue management as per CAR-OPS 1 subpart Q.


---

## 7.3.2 - Processes

# Processes

Fatigue Risk Management (FRM) includes processes for:

  * **Development of Work schedules (rosters)** that are compliant with the prescriptive limitation regulations to ensure that scientific principles are considered.

  * **Hazard identification** : Identification of fatigue hazards mainly through reactive processes, including data collected through existing safety reporting mechanisms.

  * **Risk assessment and mitigation**. Fatigue related risks are assessed and mitigated using SMS processes.

  * **FRM Safety Assurance.** SMS Safety performance indicators are established and monitored.

  * **FRM Promotion.** Training on fatigue management and fatigue related safety information is provided in internal communication.





---

## 7.3.3 - Scientific Principles and Knowledge

# Scientific Principles and Knowledge

Fatigue results in a reduced ability to carry out operational duties and can be considered an imbalance between:

  * the physical and mental demands of all waking activities (not only duty demands); and

  * recovery from those demands, which (except for recovery from muscle fatigue) requires sleep.




**Regulations are established based upon scientific principles for the purpose of managing fatigue.**

Two areas of science are central to this and are the focus of this chapter.

  1. Sleep science — particularly the effects of not getting enough sleep (on one night or across multiple nights), and how to recover from sleep loss;

  2. Circadian rhythms — daily cycles in physiology and behaviour that are driven by the circadian clock (a pacemaker in the brain). Aspects of physiology and behaviour that show circadian rhythms include:

     1. subjective feelings of alertness and sleepiness;

     2. ability to perform mental and physical work; and

     3. ability to fall asleep and stay asleep (sleep propensity).




These basic principles relate to: 1) the need for sleep; 2) sleep loss and recovery; 3) circadian effects on sleep and performance; and 4) the influence of workload, and can be summarized as:

  1. Periods of wake need to be limited. Getting enough sleep (both quantity and quality) on a regular basis is essential for restoring the brain and body.

  2. Reducing the amount or the quality of sleep, even for a single night, decreases the ability to function and increases sleepiness the next day.

  3. The circadian body clock affects the timing and quality of sleep and produces daily highs and lows in performance on various tasks.

  4. Workload can contribute to an individual’s level of fatigue. Low workload may unmask physiological sleepiness while high workload may exceed the capacity of a fatigued individual.




CRM training for flight and cabin crew include training on fatigue (refer _[OM-D Appendix A](javascript:void\(0\);)_ and _[OM-D Appendix B](javascript:void\(0\);)_).


---

## 7.3.4 - Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment 

# Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Process

Fatigue can contribute to accidents and incidents in several ways due to its direct impact on human performance.

  * Higher propensity of making errors

  * Reduction in situational awareness

  * Slower reaction time

  * Difficulty in decision making

  * Reduced communication

  * Misperception of risk




Fatigue hazards are identified mainly through reactive processes, inlcuding data collected through safety reporting systems described in _[OM-A 2.3.8](javascript:void\(0\);)_ (and Safety Management System Manual Section 7). These include Fatigue Reports, Air Safety Reports, Cabin Safety Reports, and Safety Confidential Reports.

Safety Risk Assessment process is described in _[OM-A 2.3.6](javascript:void\(0\);)_ (and Safety Management System Section 8).


---

## 7.3.5 - Risk Mitigation Process

# Risk Mitigation Process

Fatigue risk are managed by limiting the hours of duty and commensurately providing for sufficient non-duty periods in accrodance with the stipulated presciptive regulations. However, there may still remain a need for additional mitigations and controls due to any unique operating environment and workload considerations, or other factors.

Where a fatigue risk is identified while complying with prescribed limits, appropriate mitigations and controls shall be identified in accordance with the Company SMS practices.

**Fatigue related risks are managed with the Company's Safety Management System. Any supplemental mitigation required for fatigue hazards shall be documented to demonstrably control the fatigue related risks.**

SMS processes require that such risk mitigations are regularly reviewed and assessed to ensure their desired outcome continues.

Safety Risk Assessment process is described in _[OM-A 2.3.6](javascript:void\(0\);)_ (and Safety Management System Section 8).


---

## 7.3.6 - FRM Safety Assurance Processes

# FRM Safety Assurance Processes

Safety assurance consists of processes and activities undertaken by Oman Air to determine whether the SMS is operating according to expectations and requirements. This involves continuous monitoring of internal processes as well as operating environment to detect changes or deviations that may introduce emerging safety risks or the degradation of existing risk controls. Such changes or deviations may then be addressed together with the safety risk management process.

Refer _[OM-A 2.3.7](javascript:void\(0\);)_ for description of Safety Performance Monitoring Process.


---

## 7.3.7 - FRM Promotion Processes

# FRM Promotion Processes

Detailed description of Company's Safety Promotion process under the SMS is given in Safety Management System Manual Section 11.

FRM Promotion inlcudes also:

  * **Crew Training:** Crew are trained on fatigue as part of CRM training.

  * **Staff Traing:** Staff directly involved in crew planning and control activitivies are trained for use of the computerised system (AIMS) and are made aware of the related limits, requirements and provisions.





---

## 8 - Operating Procedures

# General

Operating Procedures are fundamental for achieving safety and efficiency in flight operations activities. In achieving these twin objectives Human Performance plays a very important role. This varies from individual to individual. In order to compensate for different levels of human ability to perform and assimilate, it is essential to standardise and organise the work practices in the cockpit for the crew individually and as a team to carry out their functions efficiently. With this sole purpose of achieving uniformity and ease of operation, Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) are established. They are the tools to ensure:

  1. Safe and efficient operation;

  2. Avoidance of situations leading to incidents;

  3. A step by step guidance for operations;

  4. Standardised means of carrying out the mission objectives; and

  5. A solid tool for training, checking and line flying.




Oman Air ensures compliance with, operating limitations, as defined by the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) and established by the CAA for each aircraft type used in operations.

All flight crews are required to follow the principles of good CRM throughout the execution of all flight duties. This includes exhibiting leadership, team building, conflict resolution, Common language of communication and other aspects of positive human performance. These principles shall be applied by all flight crews during line operations.

For flight techniques, performance data for all phases of flight, specific policies and procedures, refer to relevant aeroplane type AFMs, FCOMs, and FCTMs.


---

## 9 - Dangerous Goods and Weapons

# General/Policy

Dangerous Goods are defined as those articles and substances which are capable of posing a hazard to health, safety or property or the environment and which are shown in the list of dangerous goods as prescribed in the IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (DGR). These regulations provide internationally agreed standards and practices on preparation, handling and storage of Dangerous Goods so that they can be safely transported by air.

If applicable requirements are not met, it can present a serious risk to the safety of the aircraft, passengers, crew and the ground staff. It is also a criminal offence to accept or carry Dangerous Goods that are in breach of the regulation. The action for prosecution on those offences can take place in any of the countries that have adopted ICAO Annex 18. It is therefore essential that all concerned are aware of the laid down instructions in this regard for necessary compliance.

As per CAA regulations, Operator shall take all reasonable measures to ensure that no person offers or accepts Dangerous Goods for transport by air, unless the person has been trained and the goods are properly identified, classified, documented, certificated, described, packaged, marked labelled and in a fit condition for transport as required by ICAO technical instructions and IATA DGR.

Since Oman Air has adopted all the ICAO technical instructions set forth for the safe handling and transportation of Dangerous Goods and CAR requirements, Oman Air has been granted approval by the CAA to transport Dangerous Goods as per Civil Aviation Law (Royal Decree 76/2019) Article (27), CAA regulations and IATA DGR. Approval from the CAA is mandatory to transport Dangerous Goods and the same is indicated in Oman Air Operations Specifications document which is carried on board.

The IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations (IATA DGR), is used as the primary source of information to enable compliance with the Dangerous Goods requirements.

Access to CAA regulations is available via internet through the CAA website www.caa.gov.om.

Additional Information, instructions and general guidance on the transport of Dangerous Goods is given in the Cabin Safety Manual ([CSM Section 6](javascript:void\(0\);)).

Detailed information, instructions and general guidance on the transport of Dangerous Goods is given in Oman Air Dangerous Goods Manual.

The Ground Operations Department is responsible to ensure that appropriate approval for carriage of dangerous goods and weapons, ammunition etc. are in place.


---

## 10 - Security

# Introduction

This section has been developed from the CAA approved Oman Air Security Programme and contains security instructions and guidance for Flight Operations Personnel.


---

## 11 - Handling, Notifying and Reporting Occurences

# Handling, Notifying and Reporting Occurrences

**_Guidelines to Crew_**

  1. Notify the Company.

  2. Talk to NO ONE until you have had a chance to obtain representation. Local law Enforcement Personnel have a right to ask for a statement. However, none of these organizations have a right to demand a statement before you have had a chance to obtain representation.

  3. You are obligated to aid relevant Authorities in their investigation of an accident or incident, but in reasonable manner and time frame to ensure that your rights are protected. Someone other than you should determine that you are physically and mentally competent to answer questions in an intelligent manner. Be alert to the fact that you might be in shock and not know it.

  4. Notwithstanding paragraph (2.), do not talk to the authorities even casually or informally, until advised to do so by your attorney. You must show the authorities your certificate if asked, but never relinquish the certificate to any investigator. You are not allowed to attend any judicial hearings unless served with a subpoena.

  5. If you are confronted by the Local Law Enforcement Agencies be aware that you have the same rights as any citizen. If they charge you, you have the right to a lawyer; if they do not charge, there is no obligation to make a statement. In a foreign country you are afforded only those rights granted to citizens of that country. Do not demand your rights. Ask that your Company or Embassy be notified. Ask if you can have an attorney, but do not press. Make any statement short, concise, to the point and without opinions, conclusion or speculation. Try to avoid signing a statement, testifying under oath or giving a blood or urine sample until you have spoken with your representative or a Company Authority.

  6. Make no statements to the Press.

  7. Prepare ALL written statements with the help of an attorney; a hastily written statement could be taken out of context and used against you at a later date.

  8. In the event you agree to an informal interview/ inquiry, you should secure a written agreement from the authorities representative that nothing you say will be used against you in a certificate action or civil penalty proceeding. 

  9. Preserve all flight records and any other items of evidence relevant to the incident. Record the names, addresses and telephone numbers of all witnesses, including hostile or unfriendly witnesses. Do not forget to make a log book entry.





---

## 12 - Rules of the Air

# Rules of the Air

A full description of the Rules of Air as contained in ICAO Annex 2 is available in Oman Air Jeppesen Airway Manual Air Traffic Control Section. Regional Supplementary Procedures, deviations from Annex 2 and state specific procedures and requirements, are also available in Jeppesen Airway Manuals.

For details refer to the current updated Jeppesen Manual.

**_Visual and Instrument Flight Rules_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 4 & Chapter 5

**_Territorial Application of the Rules of the Air_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 2, Regional Procedures (Doc 7030) and State Rules & Procedures

**_Communication Procedures_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control; Enroute;** and **Emergency** \- 6 Communication Failure

**_Interception of Civil Aeroplanes_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 3 & Appendix 2; and **Emergency** \- 7 Interception

**_Radio Listening Watch_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control**

**_Signals_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 3 & Appendix 1

**_Time System_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 3

**_ATC Clearance, Adherence to Flight Plan and Position Reports_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- ICAO Rules of the Air - Chapter 3

**_Visual Signals - Restricted, Prohibited or Danger Area_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- Chapter 3 and Appendix 1

**_Procedures for Pilots Observing an Accident or Receiving a Distress Transmission_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Emergency** \- 8 Search and Rescue

**_Ground/Air Visual Codes for use by Survivors_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Emergency** \- 8 Search and Rescue

**_Distress and Urgency Signals_**

Refer Jeppesen Manual **Air Traffic Control** \- Chapter 3 & Appendix 1; and **Emergency** 2.5 Distress & Urgency Signals & 5 Distress and Urgency Radiotelephony Communication Procedures


---

## 13 - Leasing

# Terminology

**Dry Lease:** is when the aeroplane is operated under the AOC or authorisation of the lessee.

**Wet Lease:** is when the aeroplane is operated under the AOC or authorisation of the Lessor.

**Damp Lease:** is when the aeroplane is operated under the AOC or authorization of the lessor with partial crew.

**Lessor:** means a person or organisation which lets an aircraft for lease. The party furnishing the aircraft under a lease.

**Lessee:** means a person or organization which hold the aircraft by lease. The party using the aircraft under the provisions of a lease.

**Lease** : an agreement by a person (the lessor) to furnish an aircraft to another person (the lessee) to be used for compensation or hire purposes for a period or a defined number of flights.


---

## 14 - Freighter Operations

# Introduction

This section provides additional information and instuctions to operate freighter aircraft (all cargo).

The section adds, amends, replaces and/or supersedes information found in other sections of the Operations Manual Part A.
